title: Daumerlings Wanderschaft
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 45. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The forty-fifth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 45), and not to be confused with Daumesdick (KHM 37): here a tailor’s thumb-sized son sets off to see the world with a darning-needle for a sword. He hires himself out, helps a band of robbers plunder a king’s treasury, is swallowed by a cow and later snapped up by a fox, and by his wits comes safely home again — where his father proves he is worth more than a whole yard of hens. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gerathen, muß, faßte, ward, todt, gethan, Thier, Thüre, Thaler, wußte, gerieth, theilten, Wirthin and Vortheil.

sentences.t: Ein Schneider hatte einen Sohn, der war klein gerathen und nicht größer als ein Daumen, darum hieß er auch der Daumerling.
sentences.l: A tailor had a son who had turned out small and no bigger than a thumb, therefore he was also called the Daumerling.
sentences.i: A tailor had a son who had turned out so small, no bigger than a thumb, that he was called Thumbling.
sentences.n: “gerathen” is the 1857 spelling of geraten (‘to turn out’); “klein gerathen” = to have come out small. Daumerling is a diminutive of Daumen (‘thumb’): ‘little Thumb, Thumbling.’ The name is left untranslated and glossed.

sentences.t: Er hatte aber Courage im Leibe und sagte zu seinem Vater, „Vater, ich soll und muß in die Welt hinaus.“
sentences.l: But he had courage in his body and said to his father, “Father, I ought to and must go out into the world.”
sentences.i: But he had pluck in him, and said to his father, “Father, I shall and must go out into the world.”
sentences.n: “Courage” is a French loanword, ‘pluck, spirit’; “im Leibe” (dative -e) = in his body, i.e. in him. “muß” is the 1857 spelling of muss. “in die Welt hinaus” = out into the world.

sentences.t: „Recht, mein Sohn,“ sprach der Alte, nahm eine lange Stopfnadel und machte am Licht einen Knoten von Siegellack daran, „da hast du auch einen Degen mit auf den Weg.“
sentences.l: “Right, my son,” spoke the old man, took a long darning-needle and made at the candle-flame a knot of sealing-wax on it, “there you have a rapier too to take on your way.”
sentences.i: “Quite right, my son,” said the old man, took a long darning-needle and, at the candle-flame, made a knob of sealing-wax on it, “there — a sword for you to take along on the road.”
sentences.n: “der Alte” = the old man (adjective used as a noun). “am Licht” = at the light/candle-flame (the wax is softened there). A darning-needle with a sealing-wax pommel is a sword sized for a thumb-high hero — the first of the tale’s size jokes.

sentences.t: Nun wollte das Schneiderlein noch einmal mitessen und hüpfte in die Küche, um zu sehen was die Frau Mutter zu guter Letzt gekocht hätte.
sentences.l: Now the little tailor wanted to eat along once more and hopped into the kitchen, to see what the good mother had cooked at long last.
sentences.i: Now the little tailor wanted one more meal at home, and hopped into the kitchen to see what his good mother had cooked to send him off.
sentences.n: “Schneiderlein” is the diminutive of Schneider (‘little tailor’) — the story’s usual name for the hero, always neuter (das), so the pronouns are ‘it.’ “zu guter Letzt” is a set phrase, ‘at long last, as a parting treat.’ “gekocht hätte” is subjunctive of what he goes to see.

sentences.t: Es war aber eben angerichtet, und die Schüssel stand auf dem Herd.
sentences.l: But it had just been dished up, and the bowl stood on the hearth.
sentences.n: “angerichtet” is the past participle of anrichten (‘to dish up, serve’). “eben” = just now.

sentences.t: Da sprach es „Frau Mutter, was gibts heute zu essen?“
sentences.l: Then it spoke, “Mother, what is there to eat today?”
sentences.n: “es” is the little tailor (das Schneiderlein), grammatically neuter — read it as ‘he.’ “gibts” = gibt es (‘is there’), with the clitic -s; “was gibt es zu essen?” = ‘what is there to eat?’

sentences.t: „Sieh du selbst zu“ sagte die Mutter.
sentences.l: “Look yourself,” said the mother.
sentences.i: “See for yourself,” said the mother.
sentences.n: “sieh ... zu” is the separable verb zusehen (‘to look, see to it’); the imperative sieh stays up front and zu goes to the end. “du selbst” = you yourself.

sentences.t: Da sprang Daumerling auf den Herd und guckte in die Schüssel: weil er aber den Hals zu weit hineinstreckte, faßte ihn der Dampf von der Speise und trieb ihn zum Schornstein hinaus.
sentences.l: Then Daumerling sprang onto the hearth and peeped into the bowl: but because he stretched his neck in too far, the steam from the food seized him and drove him out through the chimney.
sentences.i: Then Thumbling sprang up onto the hearth and peeped into the bowl; but because he craned his neck in too far, the steam off the food caught him and carried him up the chimney and out.
sentences.n: “hineinstreckte” (hineinstrecken, to stretch/poke in) is separable. “faßte” is the 1857 spelling of fasste (‘seized’). “trieb ... hinaus” = drove out. Being so tiny, he is light enough to be carried off by rising steam — the size-jokes keep coming.

sentences.t: Eine Weile ritt er auf dem Dampf in der Luft herum, bis er endlich wieder auf die Erde herabsank.
sentences.l: For a while he rode about on the steam in the air, until at last he sank down again onto the earth.
sentences.i: For a while he rode about on the steam in the air, until at last he sank back down to the ground.
sentences.n: “ritt ... herum” (herumreiten, to ride about) is separable. “herabsank” (herabsinken, to sink down) is separable — herab marks motion downward.

sentences.t: Nun war das Schneiderlein draußen in der weiten Welt, zog umher, gieng auch bei einem Meister in die Arbeit, aber das Essen war ihm nicht gut genug.
sentences.l: Now the little tailor was out in the wide world, roamed about, went also into work with a master, but the food was not good enough for him.
sentences.i: Now the little tailor was out in the wide world; he roamed about and took service with a master, but the food was not good enough for him.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling recurs in the edition (gieng, anfieng). “zog umher” (umherziehen, to roam about) is separable. “bei einem Meister in die Arbeit gehen” = to enter a master’s service.

sentences.t: „Frau Meisterin, wenn sie uns kein besser Essen gibt,“ sagte der Daumerling, „so gehe ich fort und schreibe morgen früh mit Kreide an ihre Hausthüre Kartoffel zu viel, Fleisch zu wenig, Adies, Herr Kartoffelkönig.“
sentences.l: “Mistress, if you give us no better food,” said the Daumerling, “then I go off and write tomorrow morning with chalk on your house-door: potato too much, meat too little, goodbye, Mister Potato-King.”
sentences.i: “Mistress, if you give us no better food,” said Thumbling, “then off I go, and tomorrow morning I’ll chalk on your front door: too much potato, too little meat — goodbye, Sir Potato-King.”
sentences.n: “sie” here is the old polite third-person ‘you’ (formal address to the mistress). “Hausthüre” carries the old -th- (modern Haustür). “Adies” is a dialect/archaic form of adieu (‘goodbye’). “Kartoffelkönig” (‘potato-king’) is a coined mockery.

sentences.t: „Was willst du wohl, Grashüpfer?“ sagte die Meisterin, ward bös, ergriff einen Lappen und wollte nach ihm schlagen: mein Schneiderlein kroch behende unter den Fingerhut, guckte unten hervor und streckte der Frau Meisterin die Zunge heraus.
sentences.l: “What do you want, then, grasshopper?” said the mistress, grew angry, seized a rag and wanted to strike at him: my little tailor crept nimbly under the thimble, peeped out below, and stuck his tongue out at the mistress.
sentences.i: “And what do you want, grasshopper?” said the mistress, growing angry; she snatched up a rag and made to hit him — but my little tailor crept nimbly under the thimble, peeped out from beneath it, and stuck out his tongue at her.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became, grew’); modern German uses wurde — you will meet ward throughout Grimm. “bös” = angry (archaic short form of böse). “behende” = nimbly (archaic). A thimble is house-sized cover for a thumb-tall man — another size joke. “mein Schneiderlein”: the narrator’s fond ‘my little tailor.’

sentences.t: Sie hob den Fingerhut auf und wollte ihn packen, aber der kleine Daumerling hüpfte in die Lappen, und wie die Meisterin die Lappen auseinander warf und ihn suchte, machte er sich in den Tischritz.
sentences.l: She lifted the thimble up and wanted to grab him, but the little Daumerling hopped into the rags, and as the mistress threw the rags apart and looked for him, he made his way into the crack of the table.
sentences.i: She lifted the thimble and made to seize him, but little Thumbling hopped in among the rags, and while the mistress tossed the rags apart looking for him, he slipped into a crack in the table.
sentences.n: “hob ... auf” (aufheben, to lift up) and “warf ... auseinander” (auseinanderwerfen, to throw apart) are separable. “sich in ... machen” = to betake oneself, slip into. “Tischritz” = crack in the table (Tisch + Ritz).

sentences.t: „He, he, Frau Meisterin,“ rief er und steckte den Kopf in die Höhe, und wenn sie zuschlagen wollte, sprang er in die Schublade hinunter.
sentences.l: “Hey, hey, Mistress,” he cried and stuck his head up into the air, and whenever she wanted to strike, he sprang down into the drawer.
sentences.i: “Hey, hey, Mistress!” he cried, poking his head up; and whenever she went to strike, he sprang down into the drawer.
sentences.n: “steckte ... in die Höhe” = poked up (in die Höhe = up, aloft). “zuschlagen” = to strike, hit out. “sprang ... hinunter” (hinunterspringen, to jump down) is separable.

sentences.t: Endlich aber erwischte sie ihn doch und jagte ihn zum Haus hinaus.
sentences.l: At last, though, she caught him after all and chased him out of the house.
sentences.n: “erwischte” = caught (erwischen). “jagte ... hinaus” (hinausjagen, to chase out) is separable; “zum Haus hinaus” = out of the house. “doch” here = ‘after all, in the end.’

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein wanderte und kam in einen großen Wald: da begegnete ihm ein Haufen Räuber, die hatten vor des Königs Schatz zu bestehlen.
sentences.l: The little tailor wandered and came into a great forest: there a band of robbers met him, who had in mind to rob the king’s treasure.
sentences.i: The little tailor journeyed on and came into a great forest, where a band of robbers met him, who meant to rob the king’s treasure.
sentences.n: “begegnete ihm” — begegnen takes the dative: met him. “Haufen Räuber” = a heap/band of robbers. “hatten vor ... zu bestehlen” = had it in mind to rob (vorhaben + zu). “des Königs Schatz” is genitive, ‘the king’s treasure.’

sentences.t: Als sie das Schneiderlein sahen, dachten sie „so ein kleiner Kerl kann durch ein Schlüsselloch kriechen und uns als Dietrich dienen.“
sentences.l: When they saw the little tailor, they thought, “such a little fellow can creep through a keyhole and serve us as a picklock.”
sentences.i: When they caught sight of the little tailor, they thought, “A little fellow like that can creep through a keyhole and serve us for a skeleton key.”
sentences.n: “Dietrich” is a common noun here, ‘skeleton key, picklock’ (from the man’s name). “uns ... dienen” — dienen takes the dative, ‘serve us.’ His tininess makes him the perfect burglar’s tool.

sentences.t: „Heda,“ rief einer, „du Riese Goliath, willst du mit zur Schatzkammer gehen? du kannst dich hineinschleichen, und das Geld heraus werfen.“
sentences.l: “Hey there,” cried one, “you giant Goliath, will you come along to the treasure-chamber? you can sneak yourself in, and throw the money out.”
sentences.i: “Hey there,” cried one, “you giant Goliath, will you come along to the treasury? You can sneak in and throw the money out.”
sentences.n: Calling the thumb-sized man “Riese Goliath” (‘giant Goliath’) is heavy irony. “mit ... gehen” = to go along (mit as a bare adverb, ‘along’). “hineinschleichen” (to sneak in) and “heraus werfen” (herauswerfen, to throw out) are separable.

sentences.t: Der Daumerling besann sich, endlich sagte er „ja“ und gieng mit zu der Schatzkammer.
sentences.l: The Daumerling bethought himself, at last he said “yes” and went along to the treasure-chamber.
sentences.i: Thumbling considered a moment, at last said “yes,” and went along to the treasury.
sentences.n: “sich besinnen” = to reflect, consider. “gieng mit” = went along (mitgehen), separable; “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging.

sentences.t: Da besah er die Thüre oben und unten, ob kein Ritz darin wäre.
sentences.l: There he looked the door over, top and bottom, whether there were no crack in it.
sentences.i: There he examined the door top and bottom, to see whether there was any crack in it.
sentences.n: “besah” (besehen, to look over, inspect) is preterite. “Thüre” carries the old -th- (modern Tür). “Ritz” = crack, chink. “wäre” is subjunctive, ‘whether there might be.’

sentences.t: Nicht lange so entdeckte er einen, der breit genug war um ihn einzulassen.
sentences.l: Not long, and he discovered one that was broad enough to let him in.
sentences.i: Before long he found one broad enough to let him through.
sentences.n: “Nicht lange so ...” = ‘it was not long before ...’ “einen” refers back to Ritz (masculine): ‘one [crack].’ “einzulassen” (einlassen, to let in) is separable, here a zu-infinitive.

sentences.t: Er wollte auch gleich hindurch, aber eine von den beiden Schildwachen, die vor der Thür standen, bemerkte ihn und sprach zu der andern „was kriecht da für eine häßliche Spinne? ich will sie todt treten.“
sentences.l: He wanted to go straight through, too, but one of the two sentries who stood before the door noticed him and spoke to the other, “what ugly spider is crawling there? I will tread it dead.”
sentences.i: He wanted to slip straight through, but one of the two sentries standing at the door noticed him and said to the other, “What ugly spider is that crawling there? I’ll trample it dead.”
sentences.n: “hindurch” = through (with a verb of motion understood, ‘go through’). “was ... für eine ... Spinne” = ‘what kind of spider.’ “todt” is the 1857 spelling of tot (‘dead’). The guard mistakes the tiny man for a spider.

sentences.t: „Laß das arme Thier gehen,“ sagte die andere, „es hat dir ja nichts gethan.“
sentences.l: “Let the poor creature go,” said the other, “it has done you nothing at all.”
sentences.i: “Let the poor creature be,” said the other; “it has done you no harm.”
sentences.n: “Thier” is the 1857 spelling of Tier (‘animal, creature’), with the old -th-. “gethan” is the 1857 spelling of getan (‘done’) — the verb thun (modern tun) keeps its -th- throughout this edition. “ja” is a softening particle.

sentences.t: Nun kam der Daumerling durch den Ritz glücklich in die Schatzkammer, öffnete das Fenster, unter welchem die Räuber standen, und warf ihnen einen Thaler nach dem andern hinaus.
sentences.l: Now the Daumerling came through the crack happily into the treasure-chamber, opened the window under which the robbers stood, and threw them out one thaler after another.
sentences.i: Now Thumbling got safely through the crack into the treasury, opened the window under which the robbers were standing, and threw out one thaler after another to them.
sentences.n: “glücklich” here = ‘safely, successfully.’ “Thaler” is an old silver coin (with the old -th-). “einen ... nach dem andern” = one after another. “warf ... hinaus” (hinauswerfen) is separable.

sentences.t: Als das Schneiderlein in der besten Arbeit war, hörte es den König kommen, der seine Schatzkammer besehen wollte, und verkroch sich eilig.
sentences.l: When the little tailor was in the best of the work, it heard the king coming, who wanted to look over his treasure-chamber, and crept hastily into hiding.
sentences.i: When the little tailor was hard at work, he heard the king coming, who wanted to inspect his treasury, and hastily crept out of sight.
sentences.n: “in der besten Arbeit” = in the thick of the work. “verkroch sich” (sich verkriechen, to creep away and hide) is preterite. “besehen” = to look over, inspect.

sentences.t: Der König merkte daß viele harte Thaler fehlten, konnte aber nicht begreifen wer sie sollte gestohlen haben, da Schlösser und Riegel in gutem Stand waren, und alles wohl verwahrt schien.
sentences.l: The king noticed that many hard thalers were missing, but could not comprehend who should have stolen them, since locks and bolts were in good condition and everything seemed well secured.
sentences.i: The king noticed that many good thalers were missing, but could not make out who could have stolen them, since the locks and bolts were in good order and everything seemed well secured.
sentences.n: “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass (‘that’); the ß is kept throughout. “harte Thaler” = hard (solid) thalers. “fehlten” = were missing (fehlen). “begreifen” = to grasp, comprehend. “Schlösser” = locks; “verwahrt” = kept safe, secured (past participle of verwahren). “gutem” is dative, ‘in good [condition].’

sentences.t: Da gieng er wieder fort und sprach zu den zwei Wachen „habt acht, es ist einer hinter dem Geld.“
sentences.l: Then he went away again and spoke to the two guards, “take heed, there is someone after the money.”
sentences.i: Then he went off again and said to the two guards, “Be on your guard — someone is after the money.”
sentences.n: “gieng ... fort” (fortgehen, to go away) is separable. “habt acht” = ‘take heed, be watchful’ (achthaben). “einer” = ‘someone, a man’ (masc.). “hinter dem Geld [sein]” = to be after the money.

sentences.t: Als der Daumerling nun seine Arbeit von neuem anfieng, hörten sie das Geld drinnen sich regen und klingen klipp, klapp, klipp, klapp.
sentences.l: When the Daumerling now began his work anew, they heard the money inside stir and ring, click, clack, click, clack.
sentences.i: When Thumbling now set to his work again, they heard the money stirring and clinking inside — click, clack, click, clack.
sentences.n: “anfieng” is the 1857 spelling of anfing (anfangen, ‘to begin’); it is separable, here held together inside a subordinate clause. “von neuem” = anew, afresh. “drinnen” = inside. “klipp, klapp” is onomatopoeia for the clink of coins.

sentences.t: Sie sprangen geschwind hinein und wollten den Dieb greifen.
sentences.l: They sprang quickly in and wanted to seize the thief.
sentences.i: They sprang quickly inside, meaning to seize the thief.
sentences.n: “sprangen ... hinein” (hineinspringen, to jump in) is separable. “geschwind” = quick(ly).

sentences.t: Aber das Schneiderlein, das sie kommen hörte, war noch geschwinder, sprang in eine Ecke und deckte einen Thaler über sich, so daß nichts von ihm zu sehen war, dabei neckte es noch die Wachen und rief „hier bin ich.“
sentences.l: But the little tailor, who heard them coming, was quicker still, sprang into a corner and covered a thaler over himself, so that nothing of him was to be seen; and at that it still teased the guards and cried, “here I am.”
sentences.i: But the little tailor, who heard them coming, was quicker still: he sprang into a corner and pulled a thaler over himself, so that nothing of him could be seen, and even teased the guards, crying, “Here I am!”
sentences.n: “geschwinder” = quicker (comparative of geschwind). A single coin is big enough to hide under — his size is now his advantage. “neckte” = teased (necken). “so daß” = so that.

sentences.t: Die Wachen liefen dahin, wie sie aber ankamen, war es schon in eine andere Ecke unter einen Thaler gehüpft, und rief „he, hier bin ich.“
sentences.l: The guards ran there, but as they arrived, it had already hopped into another corner under a thaler, and cried, “hey, here I am.”
sentences.i: The guards ran over, but by the time they arrived, he had already hopped into another corner under a thaler, and cried, “Hey, here I am!”
sentences.n: “liefen dahin” = ran to that spot. “ankamen” = arrived (ankommen). “gehüpft” is the past participle of hüpfen (‘to hop’), forming the pluperfect ‘had hopped.’ Read the neuter “es” as ‘he.’

sentences.t: Die Wachen sprangen eilends herbei, Daumerling war aber längst in einer dritten Ecke und rief „he, hier bin ich.“
sentences.l: The guards sprang hurriedly up, but Daumerling was long since in a third corner and cried, “hey, here I am.”
sentences.i: The guards came springing over in haste, but Thumbling was long since in a third corner, crying, “Hey, here I am!”
sentences.n: “eilends” = hurriedly. “sprangen ... herbei” (herbeispringen, to come springing up) is separable. “längst” = long since, already.

sentences.t: Und so hatte es sie zu Narren und trieb sie so lange in der Schatzkammer herum, bis sie müde waren und davon giengen.
sentences.l: And so it had them for fools and drove them about in the treasure-chamber so long, until they were tired and went off.
sentences.i: And so he made fools of them, and drove them about the treasury until they were worn out and went off.
sentences.n: “jemanden zu Narren haben” = to make fools of someone (Narren = fools). “trieb ... herum” (herumtreiben, to drive about) is separable. “davon giengen” (davongehen, to go off) is separable; “giengen” is the 1857 spelling of gingen.

sentences.t: Nun warf es die Thaler nach und nach alle hinaus: den letzten schnellte es mit aller Macht, hüpfte dann selber noch behendiglich darauf und flog mit ihm durchs Fenster hinab.
sentences.l: Now it threw the thalers all out, little by little: the last it flicked with all its might, then hopped nimbly onto it itself, and flew down with it through the window.
sentences.i: Now he threw all the thalers out one by one; the last he flicked away with all his might, then nimbly hopped onto it himself and flew down with it through the window.
sentences.n: “nach und nach” = little by little, one after another. “schnellte” = flicked, sent flying (schnellen). “behendiglich” = nimbly (an extended adverb of behende). “flog ... hinab” (hinabfliegen, to fly down) is separable — riding a thrown coin, a fitting exit for a thumb-sized thief.

sentences.t: Die Räuber machten ihm große Lobsprüche, „du bist ein gewaltiger Held,“ sagten sie, „willst du unser Hauptmann werden?“
sentences.l: The robbers made him great words of praise: “you are a mighty hero,” they said, “will you become our captain?”
sentences.i: The robbers heaped praise on him: “You are a mighty hero,” they said. “Will you be our captain?”
sentences.n: “Lobsprüche” = words of praise (plural of Lobspruch). “gewaltig” = mighty, tremendous. “Hauptmann” = captain, chief.

sentences.t: Daumerling bedankte sich aber und sagte er wollte erst die Welt sehen.
sentences.l: But Daumerling gave his thanks (and declined) and said he wanted first to see the world.
sentences.i: But Thumbling thanked them and declined, saying he wanted to see the world first.
sentences.n: “sich bedanken” here carries the polite sense ‘to decline with thanks.’ “erst” = first, before anything else. “er wollte” is reported speech (‘he said he wanted’).

sentences.t: Sie theilten nun die Beute, das Schneiderlein aber verlangte nur einen Kreuzer, weil es nicht mehr tragen konnte.
sentences.l: They now divided the booty, but the little tailor demanded only one kreuzer, because it could carry no more.
sentences.i: They now shared out the booty, but the little tailor asked for only a single kreuzer, since he could carry no more.
sentences.n: “theilten” is the 1857 spelling of teilten (‘divided’), with the old -th-. “Beute” = booty, spoils. A “Kreuzer” is a small old coin; a whole thaler would be more than a thumb-sized man could lift.

sentences.t: Darauf schnallte es seinen Degen wieder um den Leib, sagte den Räubern guten Tag und nahm den Weg zwischen die Beine.
sentences.l: Thereupon it buckled its rapier around its body again, bade the robbers good day, and took the road between its legs.
sentences.i: Thereupon he buckled his sword around him again, bade the robbers good day, and took to the road.
sentences.n: “schnallte ... um” (umschnallen, to buckle on) is separable. “den Weg zwischen die Beine nehmen” is a folksy idiom, ‘to set off on foot, take to the road.’

sentences.t: Es gieng bei einigen Meistern in Arbeit, aber sie wollte ihm nicht schmecken: endlich verdingte es sich als Hausknecht in einem Gasthof.
sentences.l: It went into work with several masters, but it (the work) would not taste to him: at last it hired itself out as a house-servant in an inn.
sentences.i: He took service with several masters, but none of it was to his liking; at last he hired himself out as a house-servant at an inn.
sentences.n: “einigen Meistern” = several masters (dative plural). “sie wollte ihm nicht schmecken” — schmecken takes the dative (‘it would not please him’); “sie” refers to die Arbeit. “verdingte sich” (sich verdingen, to hire oneself out) is preterite. “Hausknecht” = house-servant, ostler; “Gasthof” = inn.

sentences.t: Die Mägde aber konnten es nicht leiden, denn ohne daß sie ihn sehen konnten sah er alles, was sie heimlich thaten, und gab bei der Herrschaft an was sie sich von den Tellern genommen und aus dem Keller für sich weggeholt hatten.
sentences.l: But the maids could not abide him, for without their being able to see him he saw everything they did secretly, and reported to the masters what they had taken for themselves from the plates and fetched away for themselves from the cellar.
sentences.i: But the maids could not stand him, for without ever being able to see him he saw everything they did in secret, and told the masters what they had taken off the plates and carried off from the cellar for themselves.
sentences.n: “Mägde” = maids (plural of Magd). “jemanden leiden können” = to be able to stand/abide someone. “thaten” is the 1857 spelling of taten (‘did,’ from thun/tun). “gab ... an” (angeben, to report, inform on) is separable. “Herrschaft” = the master(s), the household authorities. “weggeholt” = fetched away (wegholen).

sentences.t: Da sprachen sie „wart, wir wollen dirs eintränken“ und verabredeten untereinander ihm einen Schabernack anzuthun.
sentences.l: Then they said, “wait, we will pay you back for it,” and arranged among themselves to play a prank on him.
sentences.i: Then they said, “Wait — we’ll get even with you for this,” and agreed among themselves to play a trick on him.
sentences.n: “dirs” = dir es (‘pay it back to you’). “einem etwas eintränken” is an idiom, ‘to make someone pay for, get even.’ “einen Schabernack anthun” = to play a prank on (Schabernack = mischief); “anzuthun” is the zu-infinitive of anthun (modern antun), keeping the old -th-.

sentences.t: Als die eine Magd bald hernach im Garten mähte, und den Daumerling da herumspringen und an den Kräutern auf und abkriechen sah, mähte sie ihn mit dem Gras schnell zusammen, band alles in ein großes Tuch und warf es heimlich den Kühen vor.
sentences.l: When soon afterward the one maid was mowing in the garden, and saw the Daumerling springing about there and crawling up and down on the herbs, she quickly mowed him together with the grass, bound it all in a great cloth, and threw it secretly before the cows.
sentences.i: When, soon afterward, one of the maids was mowing in the garden and saw Thumbling hopping about there and crawling up and down the plants, she quickly mowed him in with the grass, tied it all up in a great cloth, and secretly threw it down before the cows.
sentences.n: “hernach” = afterward. “Kräutern” = herbs, plants (dative plural of Kraut). “auf und abkriechen” = to crawl up and down (abkriechen). “mähte ... zusammen” (zusammenmähen, to mow together) and “warf ... vor” (vorwerfen, to throw before) are separable. “Kühen” = cows (dative plural of Kuh).

sentences.t: Nun war eine große schwarze darunter, die schluckte ihn mit hinab, ohne ihm weh zu thun.
sentences.l: Now there was a big black one among them, which swallowed him down with it, without doing him harm.
sentences.i: Now there was a big black cow among them, and she swallowed him down along with the grass, without hurting him.
sentences.n: “eine große schwarze” = a big black one (i.e. cow; the noun Kuh is understood). “schluckte ... hinab” (hinabschlucken, to swallow down) is separable; “mit” = along [with the grass]. “ohne ... zu thun” = without doing (thun = tun, with old -th-). This begins the first of the two swallowings.

sentences.t: Unten gefiels ihm aber schlecht, denn es war da ganz finster und brannte auch kein Licht.
sentences.l: But down below it pleased him badly, for it was quite dark there and no light was burning either.
sentences.i: But down below he did not like it at all, for it was quite dark there and no light was burning.
sentences.n: “gefiels” = gefiel es (‘it pleased’); “es gefiel ihm schlecht” = ‘it pleased him ill,’ i.e. he did not like it (gefallen + dative). “finster” = dark, gloomy. Inside the cow’s belly all is black.

sentences.t: Als die Kuh gemelkt wurde, da rief er
sentences.l: When the cow was being milked, he called
sentences.i: When the cow was being milked, he called out:
sentences.n: “gemelkt” is the past participle of melken (‘to milk’). Trapped inside, he shouts to be heard from within the cow — the first of his cries from inside an animal.

sentences.t:
„strip, strap, stroll,
ist der Eimer bald voll?“
sentences.l:
“strip, strap, stroll,
is the pail soon full?”
sentences.n: “strip, strap, stroll” are nonsense words imitating the rhythm of milking (like ‘squirt, squirt’). “ist der Eimer bald voll?” = ‘is the pail nearly full?’ Note the vowel play strip–strap that mimics the German ablaut of a folk jingle.

sentences.t: Doch bei dem Geräusch des Melkens wurde er nicht verstanden.
sentences.l: Yet amid the noise of the milking he was not understood.
sentences.i: But amid the noise of the milking, no one made him out.
sentences.n: “Geräusch” = noise, sound. “des Melkens” is the genitive of das Melken (the gerund ‘milking’). His shout from inside the cow is drowned out.

sentences.t: Hernach trat der Hausherr in den Stall und sprach „morgen soll die Kuh da geschlachtet werden.“
sentences.l: Afterward the master of the house stepped into the stall and said, “tomorrow that cow there shall be slaughtered.”
sentences.i: Afterward the master of the house came into the stall and said, “Tomorrow that cow there is to be slaughtered.”
sentences.n: “Hausherr” = master of the house. “trat ... in” (treten, to step) is preterite. “geschlachtet werden” = to be slaughtered (passive with werden).

sentences.t: Da ward dem Daumerling angst, daß er mit heller Stimme rief „laßt mich erst heraus, ich sitze ja drin.“
sentences.l: Then the Daumerling grew afraid, so that with a clear voice he cried, “let me out first, I am sitting in here.”
sentences.i: At that Thumbling grew frightened, so that he cried out in a loud voice, “Let me out first — I’m sitting in here!”
sentences.n: “ward” again — the archaic preterite of werden. “einem wird angst” is an impersonal dative construction, ‘fear comes over someone.’ “mit heller Stimme” = in a clear/loud voice. “drin” = darin (‘in there’). He shouts again from inside the cow, now in real fear.

sentences.t: Der Herr hörte das wohl, wußte aber nicht wo die Stimme herkam.
sentences.l: The master heard that well enough, but did not know where the voice came from.
sentences.i: The master heard it well enough, but did not know where the voice was coming from.
sentences.n: “wohl” here = ‘indeed, well enough.’ “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste (‘knew’). “wo ... herkam” (herkommen, to come from) is separable — ‘where it came from.’

sentences.t: „Wo bist du?“ fragte er.
sentences.l: “Where are you?” he asked.
sentences.n: A plain question — the comedy is that the answer will make no sense to the master.

sentences.t: „In der schwarzen,“ antwortete er, aber der Herr verstand nicht was das heißen sollte und gieng fort.
sentences.l: “In the black one,” he answered, but the master did not understand what that was supposed to mean and went off.
sentences.i: “In the black one,” he answered; but the master did not understand what that was supposed to mean, and went away.
sentences.n: “der schwarzen” = the black one (the black cow; die Kuh understood, dative). “was das heißen sollte” = what that was supposed to mean. “gieng fort” (fortgehen) is separable; “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen ward die Kuh geschlachtet.
sentences.l: The next morning the cow was slaughtered.
sentences.n: “am andern Morgen” = the next morning (andern is the old spelling of anderen). “ward ... geschlachtet” — the archaic ward forms the passive ‘was slaughtered.’

sentences.t: Glücklicherweise traf bei dem Zerhacken und Zerlegen den Daumerling kein Hieb, aber er gerieth unter das Wurstfleisch.
sentences.l: Luckily, in the chopping-up and cutting-up no blow struck the Daumerling, but he got in among the sausage-meat.
sentences.i: Luckily, in all the chopping and jointing no blow struck Thumbling, but he ended up among the sausage-meat.
sentences.n: “Glücklicherweise” = luckily. “Zerhacken” (chopping up) and “Zerlegen” (cutting up, jointing) — the prefix zer- means ‘apart, to pieces.’ “gerieth” is the 1857 spelling of geriet (geraten, ‘to end up, fall into’). “Wurstfleisch” = sausage-meat.

sentences.t: Wie nun der Metzger herbeitrat und seine Arbeit anfieng, schrie er aus Leibeskräften „hackt nicht zu tief, hackt nicht zu tief, ich stecke ja drunter.“
sentences.l: As the butcher now stepped up and began his work, he screamed with all the strength of his body, “don’t chop too deep, don’t chop too deep, I am stuck underneath.”
sentences.i: As the butcher now stepped up and set to work, he screamed with all his might, “Don’t chop too deep, don’t chop too deep — I’m stuck down underneath!”
sentences.n: “Metzger” = butcher. “herbeitrat” (herbeitreten, to step up) and “anfieng” (anfangen, to begin; 1857 for anfing) are separable. “aus Leibeskräften” = with all one’s bodily strength. “hackt” is the imperative ‘chop!’ “drunter” = darunter (‘underneath’). Another life-or-death cry from inside the meat.

sentences.t: Vor dem Lärmen der Hackmesser hörte das kein Mensch.
sentences.l: For the din of the chopping-knives, no one heard that.
sentences.i: For the din of the cleavers, not a soul heard him.
sentences.n: “Vor” here = ‘on account of, for’ (Vor dem Lärmen = because of the noise). “Hackmesser” = chopping-knives, cleavers. “kein Mensch” = no one, not a soul.

sentences.t: Nun hatte der arme Daumerling seine Noth, aber die Noth macht Beine, und da sprang er so behend zwischen den Hackmessern durch, daß ihn keins anrührte, und er mit heiler Haut davon kam.
sentences.l: Now the poor Daumerling had his distress, but distress makes legs, and so he sprang so nimbly between the chopping-knives that none of them touched him, and he got away with a whole skin.
sentences.i: Now poor Thumbling was in a sad plight, but need lends wings, and he sprang so nimbly among the cleavers that not one of them touched him, and he got off with a whole skin.
sentences.n: “Noth” is the 1857 spelling of Not (‘need, distress’), with the old -th-. “die Noth macht Beine” is a proverb, ‘need makes legs,’ i.e. necessity spurs you on. “behend” = nimbly. “anrührte” (anrühren, to touch) is separable. “mit heiler Haut davonkommen” = to escape unharmed, ‘get off with a whole skin.’

sentences.t: Aber entspringen konnte er auch nicht: es war keine andere Auskunft, er mußte sich mit den Speckbrocken in eine Blutwurst hinunter stopfen lassen.
sentences.l: But escape he could not either: there was no other recourse, he had to let himself be stuffed down, together with the lumps of bacon, into a blood-sausage.
sentences.i: But he could not escape either; there was nothing else for it — he had to let himself be stuffed, along with the lumps of bacon, down into a blood-sausage.
sentences.n: “entspringen” = to escape, get away. “Auskunft” here has the old sense ‘recourse, way out.’ “muß”/“mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste. “Speckbrocken” = lumps of bacon fat. “sich ... stopfen lassen” = to let oneself be stuffed (stopfen). This begins his time trapped inside the sausage.

sentences.t: Da war das Quartier etwas enge, und dazu ward er noch in den Schornstein zum Räuchern aufgehängt, wo ihm Zeit und Weile gewaltig lang wurde.
sentences.l: There the quarters were somewhat narrow, and on top of that he was hung up in the chimney for smoking, where time and while grew tremendously long for him.
sentences.i: There the quarters were rather cramped, and on top of that he was hung up in the chimney to be smoked, where time hung terribly heavy on him.
sentences.n: “ward” again — archaic werden. “Quartier” = lodging, quarters. “enge” = narrow (old inflected form of eng). “zum Räuchern” = for smoking/curing (räuchern). “aufgehängt” = hung up (aufhängen). “Zeit und Weile wird einem lang” = time hangs heavy on one (an idiom).

sentences.t: Endlich im Winter wurde er herunter geholt, weil die Wurst einem Gast sollte vorgesetzt werden.
sentences.l: At last in winter he was taken down, because the sausage was to be set before a guest.
sentences.i: At last, in winter, he was fetched down, because the sausage was to be served to a guest.
sentences.n: “wurde ... geholt” (herunterholen, to fetch down) — herunter here written separately. “einem Gast” = to a guest (dative). “vorgesetzt werden” = to be set before, served (vorsetzen).

sentences.t: Als nun die Frau Wirthin die Wurst in Scheiben schnitt, nahm er sich in acht, daß er den Kopf nicht zu weit vorstreckte, damit ihm nicht etwa der Hals mit abgeschnitten würde: endlich ersah er seinen Vortheil, machte sich Luft und sprang heraus.
sentences.l: When the landlady now cut the sausage into slices, he took care that he did not stick his head out too far, lest perhaps his neck should be cut off along with it: at last he spied his chance, made himself room, and sprang out.
sentences.i: When the landlady now sliced the sausage, he took good care not to poke his head out too far, lest his neck should be sliced off along with it; at last he spied his chance, made himself room, and sprang out.
sentences.n: “Wirthin” is the 1857 spelling of Wirtin (‘landlady’), with the old -th-. “sich in acht nehmen” = to take care, be on one’s guard. “vorstreckte” (vorstrecken, to stick out) is separable. “mit abgeschnitten würde” = should be cut off along with it (abschneiden). “ersah ... seinen Vortheil” = spied his advantage/chance (Vortheil, 1857 for Vorteil). “machte sich Luft” = made room for himself.

sentences.t: In dem Hause aber, wo es ihm so übel ergangen war, wollte das Schneiderlein nicht länger mehr bleiben, sondern begab sich gleich wieder auf die Wanderung.
sentences.l: But in the house where it had fared so ill, the little tailor would not stay any longer, but betook itself at once again on the wandering.
sentences.i: But in the house where he had fared so ill, the little tailor would stay no longer, and set out again at once on his travels.
sentences.n: “Hause” has the old dative -e. “übel ergangen” = fared badly (ergehen; übel = ill, badly). “nicht länger mehr” = no longer. “begab sich auf die Wanderung” (sich begeben, to betake oneself) = set out on his travels.

sentences.t: Doch seine Freiheit dauerte nicht lange.
sentences.l: Yet his freedom did not last long.
sentences.n: “Freiheit” = freedom. A short, ominous sentence setting up the next capture.

sentences.t: Auf dem offenen Feld kam es einem Fuchs in den Weg, der schnappte es in Gedanken auf.
sentences.l: On the open field it came into the way of a fox, who snapped it up in his thoughts (absent-mindedly).
sentences.i: On the open field he crossed the path of a fox, who snapped him up without a second thought.
sentences.n: “einem ... in den Weg kommen” = to cross someone’s path (dative). “schnappte ... auf” (aufschnappen, to snap up) is separable. “in Gedanken” = absent-mindedly, without thinking. Here the swallower is a Fuchs (fox), not a wolf.

sentences.t: „Ei, Herr Fuchs,“ riefs Schneiderlein, „ich bins ja, der in eurem Hals steckt, laßt mich wieder frei.“
sentences.l: “Ah, Mister Fox,” cried the little tailor, “it is I who am stuck in your throat, let me free again.”
sentences.i: “Why, Master Fox,” cried the little tailor, “it’s me stuck in your throat — let me go free again!”
sentences.n: “riefs” = rief ’s = rief das (‘the little tailor cried’), with the clitic ’s standing for das. “ich bins” = ich bin es (‘it is I’). “eurem” is the old polite plural ‘your’ (addressing the fox respectfully). Once more he speaks from inside the animal that has swallowed him.

sentences.t: „Du hast recht,“ antwortete der Fuchs, „an dir habe ich doch so viel als nichts; versprichst du mir die Hühner in deines Vaters Hof, so will ich dich loslassen.“
sentences.l: “You are right,” answered the fox, “in you I have as good as nothing; if you promise me the hens in your father’s yard, then I will let you go.”
sentences.i: “You are right,” answered the fox. “There’s next to nothing to you; if you promise me the hens in your father’s yard, I’ll let you go.”
sentences.n: “an dir habe ich so viel als nichts” = ‘in you I have as good as nothing’ (you’re not worth eating). “Hühner” = hens (plural of Huhn). “deines Vaters” is genitive (‘your father’s’). “loslassen” = to let go, release.

sentences.t: „Von Herzen gern,“ antwortete der Daumerling, „die Hühner sollst du alle haben, das gelobe ich dir.“
sentences.l: “Gladly, from the heart,” answered the Daumerling, “you shall have all the hens, that I vow to you.”
sentences.i: “Gladly, with all my heart,” answered Thumbling. “You shall have all the hens — that I promise you.”
sentences.n: “von Herzen gern” = ‘gladly, with all one’s heart’ (a set phrase). “gelobe” = vow, solemnly promise (geloben). Note that he blithely promises away his own father’s hens.

sentences.t: Da ließ ihn der Fuchs wieder los und trug ihn selber heim.
sentences.l: Then the fox let him go again and carried him home himself.
sentences.n: “ließ ... los” (loslassen, to let go) is separable. “selber” = (him)self. “trug ... heim” (heimtragen, to carry home) is separable.

sentences.t: Als der Vater sein liebes Söhnlein wieder sah, gab er dem Fuchs gerne alle die Hühner die er hatte.
sentences.l: When the father saw his dear little son again, he gladly gave the fox all the hens he had.
sentences.i: When the father saw his dear little son again, he gladly gave the fox all the hens he had.
sentences.n: “Söhnlein” = little son (diminutive of Sohn). “dem Fuchs” is dative (‘to the fox’). The father values his tiny son above his whole flock — the point the last exchange will make explicit.

sentences.t: „Dafür bring ich dir auch ein schön Stück Geld mit“ sprach der Daumerling und reichte ihm den Kreuzer, den er auf seiner Wanderschaft erworben hatte.
sentences.l: “For that I bring you a fine piece of money too,” spoke the Daumerling, and handed him the kreuzer that he had earned on his travels.
sentences.i: “And in return I’ve brought you a fine bit of money too,” said Thumbling, and handed him the kreuzer he had earned on his travels.
sentences.n: “bring ... mit” (mitbringen, to bring along) is separable. “schön Stück Geld” = a fine bit of money (uninflected schön is an older colloquial usage) — comically, a single kreuzer. “Wanderschaft” = travels, journeyman’s wandering. “erworben” = earned, acquired (erwerben).

sentences.t: „Warum hat aber der Fuchs die armen Piephühner zu fressen kriegt?“
sentences.l: “But why did the fox get to eat the poor cluck-hens?”
sentences.i: “But why did the fox get to eat the poor little hens?”
sentences.n: This is a listener’s (a child’s) interruption of the tale. “Piephühner” is an affectionate, childlike word for hens (‘cluck-hens, chickies’). “kriegt” here = gekriegt (‘got’), colloquial; “zu fressen kriegen” = to get to eat (fressen is ‘to eat’ of animals).

sentences.t: „Ei, du Narr, deinem Vater wird ja wohl sein Kind lieber sein als die Hühner auf dem Hof.“
sentences.l: “Ah, you fool, to your father his child will surely be dearer than the hens in the yard.”
sentences.i: “Why, you little fool — of course your father holds his child dearer than the hens in the yard.”
sentences.n: The teller’s reply, closing the tale on its moral. “du Narr” = you fool. “deinem Vater” is dative (‘to your father’). “lieber sein als” = to be dearer than. “ja wohl” = ‘surely, of course’ (softening particles).
